literal: power, capacity, capability; from √śak 'to be able'
invariant_definition: Power, in the technical sense of an inherent capacity belonging to a substance. In two principal Vedāntic-adjacent registers: (1) Mīmāṃsā semantics — *śakti* as the word's capacity-to-denote (*abhidhā-śakti*), the meaning-relation; (2) theological — *śakti* as the divine power, especially in (a) Śākta and Trika contexts where Śakti is hypostatized as the supreme Goddess/feminine pole of the divine, and (b) Vaiṣṇava bhedābheda contexts where Kṛṣṇa's *śakti*s are systematically catalogued (*svarūpa-śakti*, *jīva-śakti*/taṭasthā, *māyā-śakti*/bahiraṅgā). All schools accept *śakti* as a real category; they differ sharply on (a) whether *śakti* is ontologically inseparable from the *śaktimat* (the power-bearer) or distinguishable from it, and (b) whether the divine *śakti* is hypostatizable as the supreme reality (Śākta-Trika: yes) or is always the property of a *śaktimat* (most Vedānta: yes).

per_school.definition: Śakti is the *vimarśa*-aspect of Śiva, inseparable from him. The Goddess Parā-vāk is the supreme Śakti through whom Śiva self-cognizes. Śiva and Śakti are two aspects (*ardhanārīśvara*) of the one supreme reality. *Svātantrya-śakti* is Śiva's sovereign freedom — the power to self-veil and self-reveal; this is the core Trika doctrine and is the source the user's thesis draws on for T7 (Maya as divine *līlā*).

Kṛṣṇa's *śakti*s are systematically threefold: *svarūpa-śakti* (or *antaraṅgā* — internal: *sandhinī*, *saṃvit*, *hlādinī*; *hlādinī* manifests as Rādhā), *taṭasthā-śakti* (marginal: *jīva*s), *bahiraṅgā-śakti* (external: *māyā*; subdivided into *guṇa-māyā* and *jīva-māyā*). Śakti is never independent of Kṛṣṇa.

*Śakti* is Viṣṇu's power of creation, sustenance, and dissolution; not hypostatized as an independent goddess. Lakṣmī (Śrī) is real and eternal but is Viṣṇu's *śakti*-personified consort, not a separate ultimate.

*Śakti* is the word's denotative capacity — the meaning-relation (*pada-padārtha-sambandha*). Bhartṛhari's *Vākyapadīya* develops *śakti* as the eternal denotative function; this directly bears on Vedānta's interpretation of *śabda-pramāṇa*.

translator_note: Translating *śakti* uniformly as 'power' or 'energy' loses crucial distinctions. The Mīmāṃsā semantic *śakti*, the Trika divine *śakti*, and the Vaiṣṇava-bhedābheda technical *śakti*s are all called the same word but operate in entirely different registers. Modern occult / yogic English usage of 'shakti' (= raw spiritual energy) is a degraded gloss far from the technical metaphysical use.
